ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chicken Manchurian; A0CKP (Chicken meat, cooked) used as closest defensible parent anchor for the primary protein component of this composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chicken Manchurian or sufficiently close prepared chicken dish concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chicken Manchurian weighs approximately 350 g, typically served as a main dish or shared starter at an Indo-Chinese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, fried (SR Legacy 05064) combined with component estimation for Manchurian sauce ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chicken Manchurian exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (05064) as the primary protein-fat anchor, with sauce component contributions (soy sauce, cornstarch, sugar, oil) estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries. The input record's per-100g values (energy 185.71 kcal, protein 12.86 g, fat 11.43 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 514.29 mg/100g is plausible given soy sauce content and is retained.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken with starchy coating and sweet soy-based sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Manchurian. GI estimated at 54 (low, at the upper boundary of the low category ≤55) based on the analogous profile of battered fried chicken with a cornstarch-thickened, sugar-containing sauce. The coating (all-purpose flour + cornstarch) contributes refined carbohydrates; the sauce sugar adds to glycaemic load. Analogous reference: battered/crumbed fried chicken GI ~54 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar preparations. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 35.0) / 100 = 18.9 (350 g serving × 10 g carbohydrate/100 g = 35 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 514 mg per 100 g driven by soy sauce; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100–150 g, request low-sodium soy sauce, and avoid adding extra condiments, as a full plate provides nearly a full day's sodium allowance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate coating (all-purpose flour and cornstarch) and sauce sugar (2.86 g/100 g) produce a low-to-medium GI (~54) and a high glycaemic load per serving (~19), accelerating postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit serving size to 150–200 g, request grilled or baked chicken to eliminate the high-GI flour-cornstarch coating, and ask for reduced sugar in the sauce to lower the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying elevates total fat (11.43 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.14 g/100 g); combined with high sodium (514 mg/100 g), this dish presents compounded cardiovascular risk through atherogenic fat intake and blood-pressure elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its combination of deep-fried fat, trans fat, and very high sodium; if consumed, a portion of no more than 100 g with a request for baked preparation and reduced-sodium sauce is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium at 514 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold, worsening tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request grilled or baked chicken to eliminate AGE formation, ask for reduced soy sauce to lower sodium, and be aware that capsicum and green chillies are nightshade vegetables that may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Manchurian is a popular Indo-Chinese dish widely served in Singapore and across South and Southeast Asia. It consists of bite-sized chicken pieces coated in a batter of all-purpose flour (maida) and cornstarch, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a pungent, umami-rich Manchurian sauce made from soy sauce, ginger, garlic, green chilli, capsicum, spring onion, vinegar, and sugar. The dish is available in two forms: dry (minimal sauce, crispy texture) and gravy (generous sauce, softer coating). The flavour profile is bold, savoury, slightly sweet, and mildly spicy, with a characteristic soy-ginger aroma. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 186 kcal, 12.9 g protein, 11.4 g fat, and 10 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 514 mg driven by soy sauce. The deep-fried preparation elevates saturated and trans fat. The glycaemic index is estimated at 54 (low boundary), with a glycaemic load of approximately 19 per standard 350 g plate, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is clinically relevant for hypertension (very high sodium), type 2 diabetes (refined carbohydrate coating, elevated GL), and cardiovascular disease (deep-fried fat, trans fat, high sodium). It is halal-suitable and dairy-free but contains gluten (wheat flour) and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chicken Manchurian?
Is Chicken Manchurian suitable for diabetics?
Is Chicken Manchurian high in sodium?
What is the difference between dry and gravy Chicken Manchurian?
What are the ingredients in Chicken Manchurian?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chicken Manchurian exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (05064) as the primary anchor, with sauce component contributions estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries. The input record's per-100g values are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken with starchy sauce. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Indo-Chinese preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or ICMR-NIN as a single dish entry as of 2024. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.14 g/100g, retained from input; plausible for deep-fried preparation using commercial vegetable oil. 3. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate at the upper boundary of the low category (≤55); no direct GI measurement for Chicken Manchurian was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. gi_category is 'low' consistent with GI 54. 4. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but does not correspond to the official HPB nutrient database; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved source. 5. cultural.primary_cuisine in the input record ('Bombay Curry And Kebabs North Indian Cuisine - South Bridge Road') appears to be a restaurant name rather than a cuisine descriptor; corrected to 'Indo-Chinese' in this output. 6. Halal flag set to true as chicken Manchurian is typically prepared without pork or alcohol in Singapore halal-certified establishments; this should be verified against the specific vendor. 7. foodex2_match_type corrected to 'closest_parent' (valid enum value) from prior draft value of 'adapted'.
